  • Import hooks for customization

Circular imports:

  • Caused by mutual dependencies
  • Solve by restructuring or delayed imports

Best practices:

  • Organize imports (stdlib → third-party → local)
  • Avoid wildcard imports
  • Use __all__ for public API
  • Clear package structure
  • Explicit imports

Understanding the module system is crucial for organizing larger Python projects.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Apply to Code Organization
  • Explore Type Systems for type hints
  • Learn Project Structure patterns

items.title: Defining Classes
items.text:
A class is a blueprint for creating objects:

class Dog:
    pass

# Create instance (object)
my_dog = Dog()
print(type(my_dog))  # <class '__main__.Dog'>

items.title: Basic Class Structure
items.text:
class Person:
    """Represents a person."""

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        """Initialize person with name and age."""
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

    def greet(self):
        """Return greeting message."""
        return f"Hello, I'm {self.name}"

# Create instances
alice = Person("Alice", 30)
bob = Person("Bob", 25)

print(alice.greet())  # Hello, I'm Alice
print(bob.greet())    # Hello, I'm Bob

items.title: Understanding self
items.text:
self refers to the instance being operated on:

class Counter:
    def __init__(self):
        self.count = 0

    def increment(self):
        self.count += 1  # Modifies this instance's count

    def get_count(self):
        return self.count

c1 = Counter()
c2 = Counter()

c1.increment()
c1.increment()
print(c1.get_count())  # 2
print(c2.get_count())  # 0 - separate instance

items.title: Instance Attributes
items.text:
Belong to individual objects:

class Car:
    def __init__(self, make, model):
        self.make = make      # Instance attribute
        self.model = model    # Instance attribute
        self.odometer = 0     # Instance attribute

car1 = Car("Toyota", "Camry")
car2 = Car("Honda", "Civic")

print(car1.make)  # Toyota
print(car2.make)  # Honda - different instance

items.title: Class Attributes
items.text:
Shared by all instances:

class Dog:
    species = "Canis familiaris"  # Class attribute

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name  # Instance attribute

dog1 = Dog("Buddy")
dog2 = Dog("Max")

print(dog1.species)  # Canis familiaris
print(dog2.species)  # Canis familiaris - same for all

# Shared across all instances
Dog.species = "Canis lupus"
print(dog1.species)  # Canis lupus
print(dog2.species)  # Canis lupus

items.title: Instance Methods
items.text:
Regular methods that operate on instance:

class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance=0):
        self.balance = balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        """Instance method - uses self."""
        self.balance += amount
        return self.balance

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if amount <= self.balance:
            self.balance -= amount
            return amount
        return 0

account = BankAccount(100)
account.deposit(50)   # 150
account.withdraw(30)  # 120

items.title: Constructor Basics
items.text:
Called when creating new instance:

class Rectangle:
    def __init__(self, width, height):
        """Initialize rectangle dimensions."""
        self.width = width
        self.height = height

    def area(self):
        return self.width * self.height

rect = Rectangle(5, 10)  # __init__ called
print(rect.area())  # 50

items.title: Default Parameters
items.text:
class User:
    def __init__(self, username, email, active=True):
        self.username = username
        self.email = email
        self.active = active

user1 = User("alice", "alice@example.com")
user2 = User("bob", "bob@example.com", active=False)

print(user1.active)  # True (default)
print(user2.active)  # False

items.title: Validation in init
items.text:
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        if not name:
            raise ValueError("Name cannot be empty")
        if age < 0:
            raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")

        self.name = name
        self.age = age

# Valid
person = Person("Alice", 30)

# Invalid
# person = Person("", 30)    # ValueError: Name cannot be empty
# person = Person("Bob", -5)  # ValueError: Age cannot be negative

items.title: Instance vs Class vs Static Methods
Instance Methods
items.text:
Most common - operate on instance data:

class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def bark(self):  # Instance method
        return f"{self.name} says woof!"

dog = Dog("Buddy")
print(dog.bark())  # Buddy says woof!

items.title: Class Methods
items.text:
Operate on class, not instance. Use @classmethod decorator:

main.pydate.py 
from date import Date

# Regular constructor
date1 = Date(2024, 1, 15)

# Class method as alternative constructor
date2 = Date.from_string("2024-01-15")

# Class method as factory
date3 = Date.today()

class Date:
    def __init__(self, year, month, day):
        self.year = year
        self.month = month
        self.day = day

    @classmethod
    def from_string(cls, date_string):
        """Alternative constructor."""
        year, month, day = map(int, date_string.split('-'))
        return cls(year, month, day)  # cls is Date class

    @classmethod
    def today(cls):
        """Factory method to create today's date."""
        import datetime
        today = datetime.date.today()
        return cls(today.year, today.month, today.day)

items.title: Static Methods
items.text:
Don't operate on instance or class. Use @staticmethod:

main.pymathutils.py 
from mathutils import MathUtils

# Call without creating instance
result = MathUtils.add(5, 3)  # 8
print(MathUtils.is_even(4))   # True

# Can also call from instance (but unnecessary)
utils = MathUtils()
result = utils.add(2, 3)  # 5

class MathUtils:
    @staticmethod
    def add(a, b):
        """Add two numbers - no need for instance or class."""
        return a + b

    @staticmethod
    def is_even(num):
        return num % 2 == 0

items.title: When to Use Each
String Representation
items.text:
main.pybook.py 
from book import Book

book = Book("1984", "George Orwell")
print(book)         # 1984 by George Orwell (uses __str__)
print(repr(book))   # Book('1984', 'George Orwell') (uses __repr__)
print([book])       # [Book('1984', 'George Orwell')] (uses __repr__)

class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author

    def __str__(self):
        """User-friendly string - used by print()."""
        return f"{self.title} by {self.author}"

    def __repr__(self):
        """Developer-friendly string - used by repr()."""
        return f"Book('{self.title}', '{self.author}')"

items.title: Comparison Operators
Arithmetic Operators
Container Methods
items.text:
main.pyplaylist.py 
from playlist import Playlist

playlist = Playlist()
playlist.add_song("Song1")
playlist.add_song("Song2")
playlist.add_song("Song3")

print(len(playlist))           # 3
print(playlist[0])             # Song1
print("Song2" in playlist)     # True
for song in playlist:          # Iteration works
    print(song)

class Playlist:
    def __init__(self):
        self.songs = []

    def add_song(self, song):
        self.songs.append(song)

    def __len__(self):
        """len() support"""
        return len(self.songs)

    def __getitem__(self, index):
        """Indexing support: playlist[0]"""
        return self.songs[index]

    def __contains__(self, song):
        """'in' operator support"""
        return song in self.songs

    def __iter__(self):
        """Iteration support"""
        return iter(self.songs)
    print(song)

items.title: Basic Inheritance
items.text:
main.pydog.pycat.pyanimal.py 
from dog import Dog
from cat import Cat

dog = Dog("Buddy")
cat = Cat("Whiskers")

print(dog.speak())  # Buddy says woof!
print(cat.speak())  # Whiskers says meow!

from animal import Animal

class Dog(Animal):  # Dog inherits from Animal
    def speak(self):  # Override parent method
        return f"{self.name} says woof!"

from animal import Animal

class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} says meow!"

class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def speak(self):
        return "Some sound"

items.title: Calling Parent Methods
items.text:
main.pycar.pyvehicle.py 
from car import Car

car = Car("Toyota", "Camry", 4)
print(car.info())  # Toyota Camry (4 doors)

from vehicle import Vehicle

class Car(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, make, model, num_doors):
        super().__init__(make, model)  # Call parent __init__
        self.num_doors = num_doors

    def info(self):
        base_info = super().info()  # Call parent method
        return f"{base_info} ({self.num_doors} doors)"

class Vehicle:
    def __init__(self, make, model):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model

    def info(self):
        return f"{self.make} {self.model}"

items.title: Multiple Inheritance
items.text:
class Flyable:
    def fly(self):
        return "Flying"

class Swimmable:
    def swim(self):
        return "Swimming"

class Duck(Flyable, Swimmable):  # Inherits from both
    def quack(self):
        return "Quack!"

duck = Duck()
print(duck.fly())    # Flying
print(duck.swim())   # Swimming
print(duck.quack())  # Quack!

items.title: Has-A Relationship
items.text:
Prefer composition over inheritance when appropriate:

main.pycar.pyengine.py 
from car import Car

car = Car("Toyota", "Camry", 200)
print(car.start())  # Toyota Camry: Engine started

from engine import Engine

class Car:
    def __init__(self, make, model, horsepower):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.engine = Engine(horsepower)  # Car HAS-AN Engine

    def start(self):
        return f"{self.make} {self.model}: {self.engine.start()}"

class Engine:
    def __init__(self, horsepower):
        self.horsepower = horsepower

    def start(self):
        return "Engine started"

items.title: Composition vs Inheritance
Method Overriding
items.text:
Different classes with same interface:

main.pycircle.pyrectangle.pyshape.py 
from circle import Circle
from rectangle import Rectangle

# Polymorphism - same interface, different implementations
shapes = [Circle(5), Rectangle(3, 4), Circle(3)]

for shape in shapes:
    print(f"Area: {shape.area()}")

# Area: 78.53975
# Area: 12
# Area: 28.27431

from shape import Shape

class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, radius):
        self.radius = radius

    def area(self):
        return 3.14159 * self.radius ** 2

from shape import Shape

class Rectangle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, width, height):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height

    def area(self):
        return self.width * self.height

class Shape:
    def area(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement")

items.title: Duck Typing
items.text:
class Dog:
    def speak(self):
        return "Woof!"

class Cat:
    def speak(self):
        return "Meow!"

class Duck:
    def speak(self):
        return "Quack!"

def make_it_speak(animal):
    """Doesn't care about type, just that it has speak()."""
    return animal.speak()


# All work - no inheritance needed
print(make_it_speak(Dog()))   # Woof!
print(make_it_speak(Cat()))   # Meow!
print(make_it_speak(Duck()))  # Quack!

# If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck

items.title: MRO Basics
items.text:
Order Python searches for methods in inheritance hierarchy:

class A:
    def method(self):
        return "A"

class B(A):
    def method(self):
        return "B"

class C(A):
    def method(self):
        return "C"

class D(B, C):  # Multiple inheritance
    pass

d = D()
print(d.method())  # B

# View MRO
print(D.__mro__)
# (<class '__main__.D'>, <class '__main__.B'>, <class '__main__.C'>,
#  <class '__main__.A'>, <class 'object'>)

items.title: C3 Linearization
items.text:
Python uses C3 algorithm for MRO:

class Base:
    def process(self):
        return "Base"

class Left(Base):
    def process(self):
        return "Left"

class Right(Base):
    def process(self):
        return "Right"

class Child(Left, Right):  # Order matters!
    pass

child = Child()
print(child.process())  # Left (first in parent list)

print(Child.mro())
# [Child, Left, Right, Base, object]

items.title: Basic Property
items.text:
Managed attribute access:

main.pyperson.py 
from person import Person

person = Person("Alice")
print(person.name)  # Calls getter: Alice
person.name = "Bob"  # Calls setter: Bob
# person.name = ""  # ValueError

class Person:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self._name = name  # "Private" convention

    @property
    def name(self):
        """Getter"""
        return self._name

    @name.setter
    def name(self, value):
        """Setter with validation"""
        if not value:
            raise ValueError("Name cannot be empty")
        self._name = value

items.title: Computed Properties
Read-Only Properties
Single Responsibility
items.text:
Each class should have one reason to change:

BadGood 
# Multiple responsibilities
class User:
    def __init__(self, name, email):
        self.name = name
        self.email = email

    def save_to_database(self):
        pass  # Database logic

    def send_email(self):
        pass  # Email logic

# Separate concerns
class User:
    def __init__(self, name, email):
        self.name = name
        self.email = email

class UserRepository:
    def save(self, user):
        pass  # Database logic

class EmailService:
    def send_email(self, user):
        pass  # Email logic

items.title: Encapsulation
items.text:
Hide internal details:

main.pybank_account.py 
from bank_account import BankAccount

account = BankAccount(100)
account.deposit(50)
print(account.get_balance())  # 150
# print(account.__balance)  # AttributeError - private

class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self.__balance = balance  # Name mangling (private)

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:
            self.__balance += amount

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if 0 < amount <= self.__balance:
            self.__balance -= amount
            return amount
        return 0

    def get_balance(self):
        return self.__balance

items.title: Favor Composition
Summary
items.text:
OOP enables organizing code into reusable, maintainable structures.

Core concepts:

  • Classes define blueprints, objects are instances
  • self refers to the instance
  • __init__ initializes new objects

Method types:

  • Instance methods: operate on instance (self)
  • Class methods: operate on class (@classmethod, cls)
  • Static methods: utility functions (@staticmethod)

Special methods:

  • __str__, __repr__: string representation
  • __eq__, __lt__: comparisons
  • __add__, __mul__: arithmetic
  • __len__, __getitem__: containers

Inheritance and composition:

  • Inheritance: "is-a" relationship
  • Composition: "has-a" relationship
  • Prefer composition for flexibility

Design principles:

  • Single responsibility
  • Encapsulation
  • Polymorphism through duck typing

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Explore decorators in Advanced Functions
  • Learn lazy evaluation with Iterators and Generators
  • Manage resources with Context Managers

items.title: About This Section
items.text:
This section covers the core building blocks of Python programming. These are the foundational elements you'll use in every Python program - from basic syntax and data types to functions and file operations. Mastering these fundamentals is essential before moving to more advanced concepts.

The focus is on understanding not just how to use these features, but why they work the way they do and when to apply them effectively.

items.title: Syntax and Data Types
items.text: Variables, basic data types (int, float, str, bool, None), operators (arithmetic, comparison, logical), comments, naming conventions, and basic I/O operations. The foundation of writing any Python code.

items.title: Control Flow
items.text: Conditional statements (if/elif/else), loops (for, while), match statements, and control keywords (break, continue, pass). How to control program execution flow and make decisions in code.

items.title: Data Structures
items.text: Deep understanding of Python's built-in data structures: lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets. Their characteristics, operations, performance implications, and when to use each one.

items.title: Functions
items.text: Defining functions, parameters and arguments, return values, scope and namespaces, default arguments, *args and **kwargs, and function documentation. Building reusable code blocks.

items.title: File Operations
items.text: Reading and writing files, working with different file formats (text, CSV, JSON), path handling with pathlib, context managers for file handling, and basic file system operations.

items.title: Error Handling
items.text: Understanding exceptions, try/except blocks, catching specific exceptions, finally clause, raising exceptions, and common built-in exceptions. Handling errors gracefully in your programs.

items.title: Modules and Imports
items.text: Using modules, import statements, import variations (from...import, import...as), understanding name, creating your own modules, and organizing code into files.

items.title: The if Statement
items.text:
Execute code only when a condition is True:

age = 18
if age >= 18:
    print("You are an adult")
    print("You can vote")

Indentation is syntax: Python uses indentation to define code blocks (4 spaces is standard).

# Wrong - IndentationError
if age >= 18:
print("Adult")

# Right
if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")

items.title: if-else
items.text:
Execute different code based on condition:

age = 15
if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")
else:
    print("Minor")

items.title: if-elif-else
items.text:
Multiple conditions evaluated in order:

score = 85

if score >= 90:
    grade = 'A'
elif score >= 80:
    grade = 'B'
elif score >= 70:
    grade = 'C'
elif score >= 60:
    grade = 'D'
else:
    grade = 'F'

print(f"Grade: {grade}")

Order matters: First matching condition wins:

x = 75

# This works
if x >= 70:
    print("C or better")  # Executes
elif x >= 80:
    print("B or better")  # Never checked

# Better structure
if x >= 80:
    print("B or better")
elif x >= 70:
    print("C or better")

items.title: Nested Conditions
items.text:
age = 25
has_license = True

if age >= 18:
    if has_license:
        print("Can drive")
    else:
        print("Cannot drive - no license")
else:
    print("Cannot drive - too young")

Prefer flat over nested when possible:

NestedFlat 
if age >= 18:
    if has_license:
        print("Can drive")
    else:
        print("No license")
else:
    print("Too young")

if age < 18:
    print("Too young")
elif not has_license:
    print("No license")
else:
    print("Can drive")

items.title: Truthiness in Conditions
items.text:
Don't need explicit comparisons:

items = [1, 2, 3]

# Verbose
if len(items) > 0:
    print("Has items")

# Pythonic
if items:
    print("Has items")

# Check for empty
if not items:
    print("Empty")

Falsy values:

  • False, None
  • 0, 0.0
  • "" (empty string)
  • [], (), {} (empty collections)

Everything else is truthy.

items.title: Chained Comparisons
items.text:
x = 5

# Verbose
if x > 0 and x < 10:
    print("Single digit positive")

# Pythonic (chained)
if 0 < x < 10:
    print("Single digit positive")

# Works with multiple chains
if 0 < x < y < 100:
    print("x and y between 0 and 100, x < y")

items.title: The for Loop
items.text:
Iterate over sequences (lists, strings, ranges, etc.):

# Iterate over list
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# Iterate over string
for char in "Python":
    print(char)

# Iterate over range
for i in range(5):  # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
    print(i)

# Range with start and end
for i in range(1, 6):  # 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
    print(i)

# Range with step
for i in range(0, 10, 2):  # 0, 2, 4, 6, 8
    print(i)

# Backwards
for i in range(10, 0, -1):  # 10, 9, 8, ..., 1
    print(i)

items.title: Iterating with Index
items.text:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Non-Pythonic
for i in range(len(fruits)):
    print(i, fruits[i])

# Pythonic - use enumerate()
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(i, fruit)

# Start index at different value
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits, start=1):
    print(i, fruit)  # 1 apple, 2 banana, 3 cherry

items.title: Iterating Multiple Sequences
items.text:
names = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie']
ages = [25, 30, 35]

# Use zip() to iterate in parallel
for name, age in zip(names, ages):
    print(f"{name} is {age} years old")

# zip stops at shortest sequence
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
letters = ['a', 'b', 'c']
for num, letter in zip(numbers, letters):
    print(num, letter)  # Only pairs (1,a), (2,b), (3,c)

items.title: Iterating Dictionaries
items.text:
person = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25, 'city': 'NYC'}

# Iterate keys (default)
for key in person:
    print(key)

# Iterate keys explicitly
for key in person.keys():
    print(key)

# Iterate values
for value in person.values():
    print(value)

# Iterate key-value pairs
for key, value in person.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

items.title: The while Loop
items.text:
Repeat while condition is True:

count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Common pattern: input validation
while True:
    response = input("Enter 'yes' or 'no': ").lower()
    if response in ['yes', 'no']:
        break
    print("Invalid input, try again")

Warning: Ensure the condition eventually becomes False to avoid infinite loops:

# Infinite loop - condition never becomes False
while True:
    print("Forever!")  # Ctrl+C to stop

# Good - condition will become False
x = 10
while x > 0:
    print(x)
    x -= 1

items.title: break - Exit Loop
items.text:
Immediately exit the innermost loop:

# Find first even number
numbers = [1, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8]
for num in numbers:
    if num % 2 == 0:
        print(f"Found even number: {num}")
        break  # Stop searching

# Exit while loop
while True:
    response = input("Continue? (y/n): ")
    if response.lower() == 'n':
        break

items.title: continue - Skip Iteration
items.text:
Skip rest of current iteration, move to next:

# Print only positive numbers
numbers = [5, -2, 8, -1, 3, -7]
for num in numbers:
    if num < 0:
        continue  # Skip negative numbers
    print(num)  # Only positive numbers printed

# Equivalent without continue
for num in numbers:
    if num >= 0:
        print(num)

items.title: else with Loops
items.text:
else clause executes if loop completes normally (not broken):

# Check if all numbers are positive
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
for num in numbers:
    if num < 0:
        print("Found negative number")
        break
else:
    print("All numbers are positive")  # Executes if no break

# Search pattern
target = 7
numbers = [1, 3, 5, 9]
for num in numbers:
    if num == target:
        print(f"Found {target}")
        break
else:
    print(f"{target} not found")  # Executes if not found

items.title: pass - Do Nothing
items.text:
Placeholder that does nothing:

# Empty block (syntax requires something)
for i in range(10):
    pass  # Do nothing, but valid syntax

# Stub for future implementation
def coming_soon():
    pass  # TODO: implement later

# Conditional placeholder
if condition:
    pass  # Handle this case later
else:
    do_something()

items.title: Match Statements
items.text: Python 3.10+ introduced pattern matching with match/case:

items.title: Basic Match
items.text:
status_code = 404

match status_code:
    case 200:
        print("OK")
    case 404:
        print("Not Found")
    case 500:
        print("Server Error")
    case _:  # default case
        print("Unknown status")

items.title: Pattern Matching with Values
items.text:
point = (0, 5)

match point:
    case (0, 0):
        print("Origin")
    case (0, y):
        print(f"Y-axis at {y}")
    case (x, 0):
        print(f"X-axis at {x}")
    case (x, y):
        print(f"Point at ({x}, {y})")

items.title: Match with Guards
items.text:
age = 25

match age:
    case n if n < 0:
        print("Invalid age")
    case n if n < 18:
        print("Minor")
    case n if n < 65:
        print("Adult")
    case _:
        print("Senior")

items.title: Match with Types
items.text:
def process(data):
    match data:
        case int():
            print(f"Integer: {data}")
        case str():
            print(f"String: {data}")
        case list():
            print(f"List with {len(data)} items")
        case _:
            print(f"Unknown type: {type(data)}")

Note

match is powerful but often an if-elif-else chain is simpler for basic cases.

items.title: Ternary Operator
items.text:
Conditional assignment in one line:

# Standard if-else
if age >= 18:
    status = "adult"
else:
    status = "minor"

# Ternary (conditional expression)
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"

# General form: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false
max_value = a if a > b else b

Use for simple cases only:

# Good - simple condition
message = "Pass" if score >= 60 else "Fail"

# Bad - too complex
result = "A" if score >= 90 else "B" if score >= 80 else "C" \
                if score >= 70 else "D" if score >= 60 else "F"

# Better - use regular if-elif-else for multiple conditions

items.title: Short-Circuit Evaluation
items.text:
Logical operators return values, not just booleans:

# or returns first truthy value
name = user_name or "Guest"  # Use user_name if truthy, else "Guest"

# and returns last value if all truthy
value = validate() and process() and save()

# Careful with side effects
x = 0
y = x or 10  # y is 10 (x is falsy)
z = x and 10  # z is 0 (first falsy value)

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Control flow in Python is straightforward and readable:

Conditionals:

  • if condition: - execute if True
  • if-elif-else - multiple conditions
  • Use truthiness instead of explicit comparisons
  • Chain comparisons: 0 < x < 10

Loops:

  • for item in sequence: - iterate over items
  • while condition: - repeat while True
  • Use enumerate() for indexed iteration
  • Use zip() for parallel iteration

Control keywords:

  • break - exit loop
  • continue - skip to next iteration
  • pass - do nothing (placeholder)
  • else with loops - executes if not broken

Pattern matching:

  • match/case (Python 3.10+) for complex pattern matching

Conditional expressions:

  • value = x if condition else y for simple cases

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Apply control flow with Data Structures
  • Build reusable logic with Functions
  • Handle errors gracefully with Error Handling

items.title: Creating Lists
items.text:
# Empty list
empty = []
also_empty = list()

# List literal
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
mixed = [1, "hello", 3.14, True]

# From other iterables
chars = list("abc")  # ['a', 'b', 'c']
range_list = list(range(5))  # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]

# Nested lists
matrix = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]

items.title: Accessing Elements
items.text:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry', 'date']

# Indexing (0-based)
fruits[0]   # 'apple'
fruits[2]   # 'cherry'

# Negative indexing (from end)
fruits[-1]  # 'date'
fruits[-2]  # 'cherry'

# Slicing [start:end:step]
fruits[1:3]    # ['banana', 'cherry']
fruits[:2]     # ['apple', 'banana']
fruits[2:]     # ['cherry', 'date']
fruits[::2]    # ['apple', 'cherry'] (every 2nd)
fruits[::-1]   # ['date', 'cherry', 'banana', 'apple'] (reversed)

# Length
len(fruits)  # 4

items.title: Modifying Lists
items.text:
Lists are mutable - can be changed in place:

numbers = [1, 2, 3]

# Change element
numbers[0] = 10  # [10, 2, 3]

# Change slice
numbers[1:3] = [20, 30, 40]  # [10, 20, 30, 40]

# Append (add to end)
numbers.append(50)  # [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

# Extend (add multiple items)
numbers.extend([60, 70])  # [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70]
numbers += [80]  # Same as extend

# Insert at position
numbers.insert(0, 5)  # [5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80]

# Remove by value (first occurrence)
numbers.remove(30)  # [5, 10, 20, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80]

# Remove by index (and return it)
item = numbers.pop(2)  # item=20, list=[5, 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80]
last = numbers.pop()   # last=80, list=[5, 10, 40, 50, 60, 70]

# Clear all elements
numbers.clear()  # []

# Delete with del
items = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
del items[0]     # [2, 3, 4, 5]
del items[1:3]   # [2, 5]

items.title: List Operations
items.text:
# Concatenation
[1, 2] + [3, 4]  # [1, 2, 3, 4]

# Repetition
[1, 2] * 3  # [1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2]

# Membership
2 in [1, 2, 3]  # True
5 not in [1, 2, 3]  # True

# Count occurrences
[1, 2, 2, 3, 2].count(2)  # 3

# Find index (first occurrence)
[1, 2, 3, 2].index(2)  # 1

# Sorting
numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
numbers.sort()  # Sorts in place: [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
numbers.sort(reverse=True)  # [5, 4, 3, 1, 1]

# Sort returns None!
result = numbers.sort()  # result is None

# sorted() returns new list
original = [3, 1, 4]
sorted_list = sorted(original)  # [1, 3, 4]
# original unchanged: [3, 1, 4]

# Reverse
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
numbers.reverse()  # [3, 2, 1] (in place)

# reversed() returns iterator
rev = reversed([1, 2, 3])  # iterator
list(rev)  # [3, 2, 1]

items.title: List Comprehensions
items.text:
Create lists concisely:

# Basic form: [expression for item in iterable]
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]  # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]

# With condition: [expression for item in iterable if condition]
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]  # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

# Transform and filter
words = ["hello", "world", "python"]
upper_long = [w.upper() for w in words if len(w) > 5]  # ['PYTHON']

# Nested comprehensions
matrix = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]
flattened = [item for row in matrix for item in row]  # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

items.title: Copying Lists
items.text:
original = [1, 2, 3]

# Reference (not a copy!)
ref = original
ref.append(4)
print(original)  # [1, 2, 3, 4] - modified!

# Shallow copy
copy1 = original.copy()
copy2 = original[:]
copy3 = list(original)

copy1.append(5)
print(original)  # [1, 2, 3, 4] - unchanged

# Deep copy (for nested structures)
import copy
nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = nested.copy()
deep = copy.deepcopy(nested)

shallow[0].append(99)
print(nested)  # [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4]] - inner list shared!

deep[1].append(88)
print(nested)  # [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4]] - unchanged

items.title: Creating Tuples
items.text:
# Empty tuple
empty = ()
also_empty = tuple()

# Tuple literal
numbers = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
mixed = (1, "hello", 3.14, True)

# Single element tuple (note the comma!)
single = (42,)  # Tuple with one element
not_tuple = (42)  # Just an int in parentheses

# Without parentheses (tuple packing)
coords = 10, 20  # (10, 20)

# From iterable
letters = tuple("abc")  # ('a', 'b', 'c')

# Nested tuples
nested = ((1, 2), (3, 4), (5, 6))

items.title: Accessing Tuples
items.text:
Same as lists - indexing and slicing:

point = (10, 20, 30)

point[0]   # 10
point[-1]  # 30
point[1:3] # (20, 30)

len(point)  # 3

items.title: Tuple Immutability
items.text:
Tuples cannot be modified after creation:

coords = (10, 20)

# These all fail with TypeError
coords[0] = 15  # Can't assign
coords.append(30)  # No append method
del coords[0]  # Can't delete element

# But can create new tuple
coords = (15, 20)  # Rebind name to new tuple

Info

If tuple contains mutable objects, those can be modified:

tuple_with_list = ([1, 2], [3, 4])
tuple_with_list[0].append(99)  # OK - modifying the list
print(tuple_with_list)  # ([1, 2, 99], [3, 4])

tuple_with_list[0] = [5, 6]  # TypeError - can't reassign tuple element

items.title: Tuple Operations
items.text:
# Concatenation
(1, 2) + (3, 4)  # (1, 2, 3, 4)

# Repetition
(1, 2) * 3  # (1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2)

# Membership
2 in (1, 2, 3)  # True

# Count and index
(1, 2, 2, 3).count(2)  # 2
(1, 2, 3, 2).index(2)  # 1

items.title: Tuple Unpacking
items.text:
# Basic unpacking
point = (10, 20)
x, y = point  # x=10, y=20

# Multiple assignment
a, b, c = 1, 2, 3  # a=1, b=2, c=3

# Swap values
a, b = b, a

# * for remaining items
first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4]  # first=1, rest=[2, 3, 4]
first, *middle, last = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]  # first=1, middle=[2, 3, 4], last=5

# Ignore values with _
x, _, z = (1, 2, 3)  # Ignore the 2

items.title: When to Use Tuples
items.text:
Use tuples for:

  • Fixed data that shouldn't change (e.g., coordinates, RGB colors)
  • Dictionary keys (must be immutable)
  • Returning multiple values from functions
  • Unpacking assignments

Use lists for:

  • Collections that may grow or shrink
  • Data that needs modification
  • When order matters and you need mutability

Performance: Tuples are slightly faster than lists and use less memory.

items.title: Creating Dictionaries
items.text:
# Empty dictionary
empty = {}
also_empty = dict()

# Dictionary literal
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "NYC"}

# dict() constructor
person = dict(name="Alice", age=30, city="NYC")

# From key-value pairs
pairs = [("a", 1), ("b", 2), ("c", 3)]
d = dict(pairs)  # {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}

# Dictionary comprehension
squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}  # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16}

items.title: Accessing Values
items.text:
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}

# Direct access (KeyError if missing)
person["name"]  # 'Alice'
person["city"]  # KeyError!

# get() method (returns None or default if missing)
person.get("name")  # 'Alice'
person.get("city")  # None
person.get("city", "Unknown")  # 'Unknown'

# Check if key exists
"name" in person  # True
"city" not in person  # True

items.title: Modifying Dictionaries
items.text:
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}

# Add or update
person["city"] = "NYC"  # Add new key
person["age"] = 31  # Update existing key

# Update multiple items
person.update({"age": 32, "job": "Engineer"})

# Remove by key (returns value)
age = person.pop("age")  # age=32, key removed

# Remove by key (doesn't return value)
del person["city"]

# Remove and return arbitrary item
person["x"] = 1
item = person.popitem()  # ('x', 1) in Python 3.7+

# Clear all items
person.clear()  # {}

# setdefault - get value, set if missing
counts = {}
counts.setdefault("a", 0)  # Returns 0, sets if key missing
counts.setdefault("a", 5)  # Returns 0 (already exists)

items.title: Dictionary Methods
items.text:
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "NYC"}

# Get all keys
person.keys()  # dict_keys(['name', 'age', 'city'])
list(person.keys())  # ['name', 'age', 'city']

# Get all values
person.values()  # dict_values(['Alice', 30, 'NYC'])

# Get key-value pairs
person.items()  # dict_items([('name', 'Alice'), ('age', 30), ('city', 'NYC')])

# Iterate
for key in person:
    print(key, person[key])

for key, value in person.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

# Copy (shallow)
person_copy = person.copy()

items.title: Dictionary Comprehensions
items.text:
# Basic
{x: x**2 for x in range(5)}  # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16}

# With condition
{x: x**2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}  # {0: 0, 2: 4, 4: 16, 6: 36, 8: 64}

# Swap keys and values
original = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
swapped = {v: k for k, v in original.items()}  # {1: 'a', 2: 'b'}

items.title: Dictionary Ordering
items.text:
Python 3.7+ maintains insertion order:

d = {}
d['b'] = 2
d['a'] = 1
d['c'] = 3

list(d.keys())  # ['b', 'a', 'c'] - order preserved

items.title: Nested Dictionaries
items.text:
users = {
    "alice": {"age": 30, "city": "NYC"},
    "bob": {"age": 25, "city": "LA"}
}

users["alice"]["age"]  # 30
users["charlie"] = {"age": 35, "city": "Chicago"}

items.title: Creating Sets
items.text:
# Empty set (must use set(), not {})
empty = set()

# Set literal
numbers = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}

# From iterable
letters = set("hello")  # {'h', 'e', 'l', 'o'} - duplicates removed

# Set comprehension
evens = {x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}  # {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}

Info

Sets are unordered and contain unique elements only.

{1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3}  # {1, 2, 3} - duplicates removed

items.title: Set Operations
items.text:
# Add element
numbers = {1, 2, 3}
numbers.add(4)  # {1, 2, 3, 4}
numbers.add(2)  # {1, 2, 3, 4} - no effect, already exists

# Remove element
numbers.remove(3)  # {1, 2, 4} (KeyError if not found)
numbers.discard(10)  # No error if not found

# Pop random element
item = numbers.pop()

# Clear all
numbers.clear()  # set()

# Membership (O(1) - very fast!)
3 in {1, 2, 3, 4}  # True

# Length
len({1, 2, 3})  # 3

items.title: Mathematical Set Operations
items.text:
a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
b = {3, 4, 5, 6}

# Union (all elements from both)
a | b  # {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
a.union(b)

# Intersection (elements in both)
a & b  # {3, 4}
a.intersection(b)

# Difference (in a but not in b)
a - b  # {1, 2}
a.difference(b)

# Symmetric difference (in either but not both)
a ^ b  # {1, 2, 5, 6}
a.symmetric_difference(b)

# Subset
{1, 2} < {1, 2, 3}  # True
{1, 2}.issubset({1, 2, 3})

# Superset
{1, 2, 3} > {1, 2}  # True
{1, 2, 3}.issuperset({1, 2})

# Disjoint (no common elements)
{1, 2}.isdisjoint({3, 4})  # True

items.title: Frozen Sets
items.text:
Immutable version of sets (can be dictionary keys):

fs = frozenset([1, 2, 3])

# Can't modify
fs.add(4)  # AttributeError

# Can use as dict key
d = {fs: "value"}

# Can perform read operations
fs | {4, 5}  # Returns new frozenset: frozenset({1, 2, 3, 4, 5})

items.title: Choosing the Right Data Structure
items.text:
Use Case Data Structure Why Ordered collection, need to modify List Mutable, ordered, allows duplicates Sequence of same type (large data) List Simple, efficient for homogeneous data Key-value lookups Dictionary O(1) access by key Config data that shouldn't change Dictionary Named access, clear structure Ordered collection, shouldn't change Tuple Immutable, ordered, faster than list Multiple return values Tuple Conventional, unpacks easily Unique elements, membership tests Set O(1) membership, automatic uniqueness Mathematical set operations Set Union, intersection, difference built-in

Performance comparison:

# Membership testing
'x' in my_list  # O(n) - slow
'x' in my_set   # O(1) - fast

# Lookup by key
my_dict['key']  # O(1) - fast

# Append
my_list.append(x)  # O(1) - fast

# Insert at beginning
my_list.insert(0, x)  # O(n) - slow (shifts all elements)

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Python's core data structures serve different purposes. Choose based on your needs: mutability, ordering, lookup speed, and whether you need keys or just values.

Lists:

  • Ordered, mutable sequences
  • Support indexing, slicing, sorting
  • Use for ordered collections that change

Tuples:

  • Ordered, immutable sequences
  • Faster than lists, less memory
  • Use for fixed data, multiple returns, dict keys

Dictionaries:

  • Key-value pairs, O(1) lookup
  • Ordered (Python 3.7+)
  • Use for lookups, mappings, structured data

Sets:

  • Unordered, unique elements, O(1) membership
  • Mathematical set operations
  • Use for uniqueness, membership tests, set math

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Use these structures with Functions for complex operations
  • Combine with Control Flow for data processing
  • Learn more advanced patterns in Core Concepts

items.title: What are Exceptions?
items.text:
Exceptions are runtime errors that disrupt normal program flow. When Python encounters an error, it raises an exception.

# This raises a ZeroDivisionError
result = 10 / 0

# This raises a TypeError
result = "hello" + 5

# This raises a ValueError
number = int("abc")

Unhandled Exceptions

Without error handling, exceptions crash the program:

def divide(a, b):
    return a / b

result = divide(10, 0)  # ZeroDivisionError - program stops
print("This never prints")

items.title: Basic Exception Handling
items.text:
try:
    # Code that might raise an exception
    result = 10 / 0
except:
    # Handle any exception
    print("An error occurred")

print("Program continues")

Output:

An error occurred
Program continues

items.title: Accessing Exception Details
items.text:
try:
    result = 10 / 0
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")
    print(f"Type: {type(e).__name__}")

Output:

Error: division by zero
Type: ZeroDivisionError

items.title: Why Catch Specific Exceptions?
items.text:
Catching all exceptions is too broad - handle different errors differently:

def read_number_from_file(filename):
    try:
        with open(filename, 'r') as f:
            content = f.read()
            number = int(content)
            return number
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print(f"File {filename} not found")
        return None
    except ValueError:
        print("File content is not a valid number")
        return None

items.title: Specific Exception Syntax
items.text:
try:
    # Risky operation
    result = some_operation()
except SpecificError:
    # Handle SpecificError
    handle_error()

Example:

def safe_divide(a, b):
    try:
        return a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print("Cannot divide by zero")
        return None
    except TypeError:
        print("Arguments must be numbers")
        return None

safe_divide(10, 0)      # Cannot divide by zero
safe_divide(10, "2")    # Arguments must be numbers
safe_divide(10, 2)      # 5.0

items.title: Sequential Exception Handlers
items.text:
try:
    file = open('data.txt', 'r')
    content = file.read()
    number = int(content)
    result = 100 / number
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
except ValueError:
    print("File contains invalid number")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Number cannot be zero")
finally:
    try:
        file.close()
    except:
        pass

items.title: Catching Multiple Exceptions Together
items.text:
try:
    # Operation that might fail
    result = process_data()
except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
    print(f"Invalid data: {e}")

# Equivalent to:
try:
    result = process_data()
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Invalid data: {e}")
except TypeError as e:
    print(f"Invalid data: {e}")

items.title: The else Clause
items.text:
Executes if no exception occurred:

try:
    number = int(input("Enter a number: "))
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid number")
else:
    print(f"You entered: {number}")
    # This only runs if no exception occurred

Use else for:

  • Code that should only run if try succeeded
  • Keeping try block minimal (only code that might raise exception)
try:
    file = open('data.txt', 'r')
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
else:
    # Only runs if file opened successfully
    content = file.read()
    process(content)
    file.close()

items.title: The finally Clause
items.text:
Always executes, regardless of exceptions:

try:
    file = open('data.txt', 'r')
    content = file.read()
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
finally:
    # This always runs
    print("Cleanup complete")

Use finally for:

  • Cleanup operations (closing files, connections)
  • Releasing resources
  • Operations that must execute
file = None
try:
    file = open('data.txt', 'r')
    content = file.read()
    process(content)
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
finally:
    if file:
        file.close()  # Always close the file

items.title: Complete Structure
items.text:
try:
    # Code that might raise exception
    risky_operation()
except SpecificError:
    # Handle specific error
    handle_error()
else:
    # Runs if no exception
    continue_processing()
finally:
    # Always runs
    cleanup()

items.title: Basic raise
items.text:
def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0:
        raise ValueError("Denominator cannot be zero")
    return a / b

divide(10, 0)  # ValueError: Denominator cannot be zero

items.title: Raising Without Arguments
items.text:
Re-raise the current exception:

try:
    risky_operation()
except SomeError as e:
    log_error(e)
    raise  # Re-raise the same exception

items.title: Raising Different Exception
items.text:
try:
    result = process_data()
except ValueError as e:
    # Catch one exception, raise another
    raise RuntimeError(f"Processing failed: {e}")

items.title: Exception Chaining
items.text:
Preserve original exception:

try:
    result = int("abc")
except ValueError as e:
    raise TypeError("Invalid type") from e

# Shows both exceptions:
# TypeError: Invalid type
# The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:
# ValueError: invalid literal for int()

items.title: ValueError
items.text:
Raised when operation receives correct type but inappropriate value:

int("abc")  # ValueError: invalid literal
int("123")  # OK

# Custom validation
def set_age(age):
    if age < 0:
        raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")
    return age

items.title: TypeError
items.text:
Raised when operation applied to wrong type:

"hello" + 5  # TypeError: can only concatenate str
len(5)       # TypeError: object of type 'int' has no len()

# Type checking
def add(a, b):
    if not isinstance(a, (int, float)) or not isinstance(b, (int, float)):
        raise TypeError("Arguments must be numbers")
    return a + b

items.title: KeyError
items.text:
Raised when dictionary key doesn't exist:

d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
d['c']  # KeyError: 'c'

# Safe access
try:
    value = d['c']
except KeyError:
    value = None

# Better: use get()
value = d.get('c', None)

items.title: IndexError
items.text:
Raised when sequence index is out of range:

items = [1, 2, 3]
items[5]  # IndexError: list index out of range

# Safe access
try:
    item = items[5]
except IndexError:
    item = None

items.title: FileNotFoundError
items.text:
Raised when file doesn't exist:

with open('nonexistent.txt', 'r') as f:
    # FileNotFoundError
    pass

# Handle gracefully
try:
    with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
        content = f.read()
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found, using defaults")
    content = ""

items.title: AttributeError
items.text:
Raised when attribute doesn't exist:

"hello".nonexistent_method()  # AttributeError

# Check before accessing
if hasattr(obj, 'method'):
    obj.method()

items.title: ZeroDivisionError
items.text:
Raised when dividing by zero:

10 / 0  # ZeroDivisionError

def safe_divide(a, b):
    try:
        return a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        return float('inf')  # Or None, or handle differently

items.title: Base Classes
items.text:
BaseException
├── SystemExit
├── KeyboardInterrupt
└── Exception
    ├── ValueError
    ├── TypeError
    ├── KeyError
    ├── IndexError
    ├── FileNotFoundError
    ├── ZeroDivisionError
    ├── AttributeError
    └── ... (many more)

items.title: Catching Base Exception
items.text:
# Catch all exceptions (usually too broad)
try:
    risky_code()
except Exception as e:
    # Catches most exceptions, but not SystemExit or KeyboardInterrupt
    handle_error(e)

# Catch absolutely everything (rarely needed)
try:
    risky_code()
except BaseException as e:
    # Catches everything, including Ctrl+C
    handle_error(e)

items.title: Best Practice
items.text:
Catch specific exceptions when possible:

# Good - specific
try:
    number = int(user_input)
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid number")

# Bad - too broad
try:
    number = int(user_input)
except Exception:
    print("Something went wrong")

items.title: Keep Try Blocks Small
items.text:
BadGood 
try:
    file = open('data.txt')
    content = file.read()
    data = json.loads(content)
    result = process(data)
    output = format_result(result)
    print(output)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")

try:
    file = open('data.txt')
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found")
    return

try:
    data = json.loads(file.read())
except json.JSONDecodeError:
    print("Invalid JSON")
    return

result = process(data)
output = format_result(result)
print(output)

items.title: Don't Silence Exceptions
Use Specific Exceptions
items.text:
BadGood 
if something_wrong:
    raise Exception("Error")

if value < 0:
    raise ValueError("Value must be non-negative")

items.title: Don't Use Exceptions for Control Flow
Document Exceptions
items.text:
def divide(a, b):
    """
    Divide a by b.

    Args:
        a: Numerator
        b: Denominator

    Returns:
        The quotient

    Raises:
        ValueError: If b is zero
        TypeError: If arguments are not numbers
    """
    if b == 0:
        raise ValueError("Cannot divide by zero")
    return a / b

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Proper error handling makes programs robust and easier to debug.

Basic structure:

  • try - code that might raise exception
  • except - handle specific exceptions
  • else - runs if no exception (optional)
  • finally - always runs (optional)

Key concepts:

  • Catch specific exceptions when possible
  • Use else for code that should only run on success
  • Use finally for cleanup that must always occur
  • Raise exceptions with descriptive messages

Common exceptions:

  • ValueError - wrong value
  • TypeError - wrong type
  • KeyError - missing dictionary key
  • IndexError - index out of range
  • FileNotFoundError - file doesn't exist

Best practices:

  • Keep try blocks minimal
  • Don't silence exceptions
  • Use specific exception types
  • Document what exceptions functions raise

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Organize code with Modules and Imports
  • Apply error handling in File Operations
  • Explore advanced exception patterns in Core Concepts

items.title: Basic File Reading
items.text:
# Open and read entire file
file = open('example.txt', 'r')
content = file.read()
print(content)
file.close()  # Always close files!

Warning

If an error occurs, file might not close properly.

items.title: Reading Methods
items.text:
file = open('example.txt', 'r')

# Read entire file as string
content = file.read()

# Read one line
line = file.readline()

# Read all lines into list
lines = file.readlines()

file.close()

Example:

main.pyexample.txt 
file = open('example.txt', 'r')

# read() - entire file as string
content = file.read()
print(repr(content))  # 'Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3\n'

# Go back to start
file.seek(0)

# readline() - one line at a time
line1 = file.readline()  # 'Line 1\n'
line2 = file.readline()  # 'Line 2\n'

# Go back to start
file.seek(0)

# readlines() - list of lines
lines = file.readlines()  # ['Line 1\n', 'Line 2\n', 'Line 3\n']

file.close()

# Line 1
# Line 2
# Line 3

items.title: Reading Line by Line
items.text:
More memory efficient for large files:

file = open('large_file.txt', 'r')
for line in file:
    print(line.strip())  # Remove \n
file.close()

items.title: Reading with Size Limit
items.text:
file = open('example.txt', 'r')

# Read first 10 characters
chunk = file.read(10)

# Read next 20 characters
chunk = file.read(20)

file.close()

items.title: Basic File Writing
items.text:
# Write mode (overwrites existing file)
file = open('output.txt', 'w')
file.write('Hello, World!')
file.close()

items.title: Writing Multiple Lines
items.text:
file = open('output.txt', 'w')

file.write('Line 1\n')
file.write('Line 2\n')
file.write('Line 3\n')

# Or use writelines() with a list
lines = ['Line 1\n', 'Line 2\n', 'Line 3\n']
file.writelines(lines)

file.close()

Info

write() doesn't add newlines automatically - you must include \n.

items.title: Appending to Files
items.text:
# Append mode (adds to end of file)
file = open('log.txt', 'a')
file.write('New log entry\n')
file.close()

items.title: File Modes
items.text:
Common file modes:

Mode Description Creates if Missing Truncates 'r' Read (default) No No 'w' Write Yes Yes 'a' Append Yes No 'x' Exclusive create Yes (fails if exists) No 'r+' Read and write No No 'w+' Write and read Yes Yes 'a+' Append and read Yes No

Binary modes: Add 'b' suffix:

# Read binary file
file = open('image.png', 'rb')
data = file.read()
file.close()

# Write binary file
file = open('output.bin', 'wb')
file.write(b'\x00\x01\x02\x03')
file.close()

Text vs Binary:

  • Text mode ('r', 'w'): Handles encoding, line endings automatically
  • Binary mode ('rb', 'wb'): Raw bytes, no conversion

items.title: The with Statement
items.text:
Automatically closes files, even if errors occur:

# Recommended way to work with files
with open('example.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)
# File automatically closed here

# Can access content after block
print(content)

Multiple files:

with open('input.txt', 'r') as infile, open('output.txt', 'w') as outfile:
    content = infile.read()
    outfile.write(content.upper())

items.title: Why Use with?
items.text:
# Without with - must handle errors
file = open('example.txt', 'r')
try:
    content = file.read()
finally:
    file.close()  # Ensures file closes even with errors

# With with - automatic cleanup
with open('example.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
# Automatically closes, even if exception occurs

items.title: Common Patterns
items.text:
Read entire file:

with open('file.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

Read lines:

with open('file.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:
        process(line.strip())

Write lines:

with open('output.txt', 'w') as f:
    for item in items:
        f.write(f"{item}\n")

Copy file:

with open('source.txt', 'r') as src, open('dest.txt', 'w') as dst:
    dst.write(src.read())

items.title: pathlib Module
items.text:
Modern, object-oriented path handling:

from pathlib import Path

# Create path object
path = Path('data/file.txt')

# Check if exists
if path.exists():
    print("File exists")

# Check if file or directory
path.is_file()  # True if file
path.is_dir()   # True if directory

# Get file info
path.name       # 'file.txt'
path.stem       # 'file' (without extension)
path.suffix     # '.txt'
path.parent     # Path('data')

# Read/write with Path
content = path.read_text()
path.write_text('New content')

# Binary
data = path.read_bytes()
path.write_bytes(b'\x00\x01')

items.title: Building Paths
items.text:
from pathlib import Path

# Join paths (works on all OS)
path = Path('data') / 'subdir' / 'file.txt'
# Windows: data\subdir\file.txt
# Unix: data/subdir/file.txt

# Current directory
cwd = Path.cwd()

# Home directory
home = Path.home()

# Absolute path
abs_path = path.resolve()

# Create parent directories
path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

items.title: Listing Directory Contents
items.text:
from pathlib import Path

directory = Path('.')

# List all items
for item in directory.iterdir():
    print(item)

# List files only
for file in directory.glob('*.txt'):
    print(file)

# Recursive search
for file in directory.rglob('*.py'):
    print(file)

items.title: os.path
items.text:
Still common in older code:

import os

# Join paths
path = os.path.join('data', 'file.txt')

# Check existence
os.path.exists(path)
os.path.isfile(path)
os.path.isdir(path)

# Get parts
os.path.basename(path)  # 'file.txt'
os.path.dirname(path)   # 'data'
os.path.splitext(path)  # ('data/file', '.txt')

# Absolute path
os.path.abspath(path)

items.title: Text Files
items.text:
# Simple text
with open('notes.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('This is a text file.\n')
    f.write('It contains plain text.\n')

with open('notes.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

items.title: CSV Files
items.text:
import csv

# Writing CSV
with open('data.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(['Name', 'Age', 'City'])
    writer.writerow(['Alice', 30, 'NYC'])
    writer.writerow(['Bob', 25, 'LA'])

# Reading CSV
with open('data.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.reader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row)  # ['Name', 'Age', 'City'], etc.

# Dictionary reader/writer
with open('data.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row['Name'], row['Age'])

with open('output.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    fieldnames = ['Name', 'Age', 'City']
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fieldnames)
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerow({'Name': 'Alice', 'Age': 30, 'City': 'NYC'})

items.title: JSON Files
items.text:
import json

# Writing JSON
data = {
    'name': 'Alice',
    'age': 30,
    'hobbies': ['reading', 'hiking']
}

with open('data.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

# Reading JSON
with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
    print(data['name'])  # Alice

# JSON to/from strings
json_string = json.dumps(data)
data = json.loads(json_string)

items.title: Configuration Files
items.text:
INI format:

import configparser

# Writing
config = configparser.ConfigParser()
config['DEFAULT'] = {'ServerAlive': 'yes'}
config['Database'] = {'Host': 'localhost', 'Port': '5432'}

with open('config.ini', 'w') as f:
    config.write(f)

# Reading
config = configparser.ConfigParser()
config.read('config.ini')
print(config['Database']['Host'])  # localhost

items.title: Creating Directories
items.text:
from pathlib import Path

# Create directory
Path('new_dir').mkdir()

# Create with parents (like mkdir -p)
Path('parent/child/grandchild').mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Using os
import os
os.mkdir('new_dir')
os.makedirs('parent/child/grandchild', exist_ok=True)

items.title: Deleting Files and Directories
items.text:
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

# Delete file
Path('file.txt').unlink()

# Delete empty directory
Path('empty_dir').rmdir()

# Delete directory and contents
shutil.rmtree('dir_with_contents')

# Using os
import os
os.remove('file.txt')
os.rmdir('empty_dir')

items.title: Copying and Moving
items.text:
import shutil

# Copy file
shutil.copy('source.txt', 'dest.txt')
shutil.copy2('source.txt', 'dest.txt')  # Preserves metadata

# Copy directory
shutil.copytree('source_dir', 'dest_dir')

# Move (rename)
shutil.move('old_name.txt', 'new_name.txt')
shutil.move('file.txt', 'new_directory/')

items.title: Renaming
items.text:
from pathlib import Path

# Using Path
Path('old.txt').rename('new.txt')

# Using os
import os
os.rename('old.txt', 'new.txt')

items.title: Getting File Information
items.text:
from pathlib import Path
import os

path = Path('file.txt')

# Size in bytes
size = path.stat().st_size

# Modification time
mtime = path.stat().st_mtime

# Using os
size = os.path.getsize('file.txt')
mtime = os.path.getmtime('file.txt')

# Convert timestamp to datetime
from datetime import datetime
mod_time = datetime.fromtimestamp(mtime)

items.title: Temporary Files
items.text:
import tempfile

# Temporary file
with tempfile.TemporaryFile(mode='w+') as f:
    f.write('temporary data')
    f.seek(0)
    print(f.read())
# File deleted when closed

# Named temporary file
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', delete=False) as f:
    f.write('data')
    temp_name = f.name

# File persists, but can be deleted manually
os.remove(temp_name)

# Temporary directory
with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
    print(f"Created {tmpdir}")
    # Use tmpdir...
# Directory and contents deleted

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Proper file handling is essential for data persistence, configuration, and program interaction with the file system. File operations in Python:

Reading files:

  • open('file.txt', 'r') and .read(), .readline(), .readlines()
  • Iterate over file object for line-by-line reading

Writing files:

  • open('file.txt', 'w') for write, 'a' for append
  • .write() and .writelines()

Best practices:

  • Always use with statement for automatic cleanup
  • Use pathlib.Path for path operations
  • Choose appropriate file mode

File formats:

  • CSV with csv module
  • JSON with json module
  • Configuration with configparser

File system:

  • pathlib.Path for creating, deleting, moving files/directories
  • shutil for copying and moving
  • tempfile for temporary files

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Handle file errors with Error Handling
  • Organize code with Modules and Imports
  • Explore advanced I/O in Core Concepts

items.title: Basic Function Syntax
items.text:
def function_name(parameters):
    """Docstring describing the function"""
    # Function body
    return result

Example:

def greet(name):
    """Return a greeting message."""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

# Call the function
message = greet("Alice")
print(message)  # Hello, Alice!

items.title: Functions Without Return
items.text:
If no return statement, function returns None:

def print_greeting(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")
    # No return statement

result = print_greeting("Bob")  # Prints: Hello, Bob!
print(result)  # None

items.title: Multiple Statements
items.text:
def calculate_circle_area(radius):
    """Calculate and return the area of a circle."""
    pi = 3.14159
    area = pi * radius ** 2
    return area

area = calculate_circle_area(5)
print(area)  # 78.53975

items.title: Positional Parameters
items.text:
Arguments passed by position:

def divide(a, b):
    return a / b

divide(10, 2)  # 5.0 - a=10, b=2
divide(2, 10)  # 0.2 - a=2, b=10 (order matters!)

items.title: Keyword Arguments
items.text:
Arguments passed by name:

def describe_pet(animal, name):
    print(f"I have a {animal} named {name}.")

# Positional
describe_pet("dog", "Buddy")

# Keyword (order doesn't matter)
describe_pet(name="Buddy", animal="dog")
describe_pet(animal="cat", name="Whiskers")

# Mix positional and keyword (positional must come first)
describe_pet("hamster", name="Nibbles")

items.title: Parameter vs Argument
items.text:
• Parameter: Variable in function definition
  • Argument: Actual value passed when calling
def greet(name):  # 'name' is a parameter
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

greet("Alice")  # "Alice" is an argument

items.title: Single Return Value
items.text:
def square(x):
    return x ** 2

result = square(5)  # 25

items.title: Multiple Return Values
items.text:
Functions can return multiple values as a tuple:

def min_max(numbers):
    return min(numbers), max(numbers)

minimum, maximum = min_max([1, 5, 3, 9, 2])
print(minimum, maximum)  # 1 9

# Actually returns a tuple
result = min_max([1, 5, 3, 9, 2])
print(result)  # (1, 9)
print(type(result))  # <class 'tuple'>

items.title: Early Returns
items.text:
def find_item(items, target):
    """Return index of target, or -1 if not found."""
    for i, item in enumerate(items):
        if item == target:
            return i  # Early return
    return -1  # If loop completes without finding

index = find_item([1, 2, 3, 4], 3)  # 2
index = find_item([1, 2, 3, 4], 10)  # -1

items.title: Return None Explicitly
items.text:
def process(value):
    if value < 0:
        return None  # Explicit None
    return value * 2

result = process(-5)  # None
result = process(5)   # 10

items.title: Local Scope
items.text:
Variables created inside a function are local:

def my_function():
    x = 10  # Local variable
    print(x)

my_function()  # 10
print(x)  # NameError - x doesn't exist outside function

items.title: Global Scope
items.text:
Variables defined outside functions are global:

x = 100  # Global variable

def my_function():
    print(x)  # Can read global

my_function()  # 100

items.title: Modifying Global Variables
items.text:
Use global keyword to modify global variables:

counter = 0

def increment():
    global counter  # Declare we're using global variable
    counter += 1

increment()
print(counter)  # 1

increment()
print(counter)  # 2

Without global:

counter = 0

def increment():
    counter = 1  # Creates local variable, doesn't modify global

increment()
print(counter)  # 0 (unchanged)

items.title: LEGB Rule
items.text:
Python searches for variables in this order:

  1. Local - inside current function
  2. Enclosing - in enclosing function(s)
  3. Global - module level
  4. Built-in - Python built-ins
x = "global"

def outer():
    x = "enclosing"

    def inner():
        x = "local"
        print(x)  # Searches: local → enclosing → global → built-in

    inner()  # Prints: local
    print(x)  # Prints: enclosing

outer()
print(x)  # Prints: global

items.title: Basic Default Values
items.text:
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}!"

greet("Alice")  # Hello, Alice! (uses default)
greet("Bob", "Hi")  # Hi, Bob! (overrides default)
greet("Charlie", greeting="Hey")  # Hey, Charlie!

items.title: Multiple Defaults
items.text:
def connect(host="localhost", port=8080, timeout=30):
    print(f"Connecting to {host}:{port} (timeout: {timeout}s)")

connect()  # All defaults
connect("example.com")  # Custom host, default port and timeout
connect("example.com", 443)  # Custom host and port
connect(port=9000)  # Custom port, keyword argument

items.title: Mutable Default Argument Trap
items.text:
Danger: Mutable defaults are created once and shared:

def add_item(item, items=[]):  # BAD - mutable default
    items.append(item)
    return items

list1 = add_item("apple")  # ['apple']
list2 = add_item("banana")  # ['apple', 'banana'] - UNEXPECTED!

Fix: Use None and create new object inside:

def add_item(item, items=None):  # GOOD
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append(item)
    return items

list1 = add_item("apple")  # ['apple']
list2 = add_item("banana")  # ['banana'] - correct!

items.title: *args - Variable Positional Arguments
items.text:
Collect extra positional arguments into a tuple:

def sum_all(*numbers):
    """Sum any number of arguments."""
    total = 0
    for num in numbers:
        total += num
    return total

sum_all(1, 2, 3)  # 6
sum_all(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)  # 15
sum_all()  # 0

# numbers is a tuple
def print_args(*args):
    print(type(args))  # <class 'tuple'>
    print(args)

print_args(1, 2, 3)  # (1, 2, 3)

items.title: Mixing Regular and *args
items.text:
def greet(greeting, *names):
    """Greet multiple people."""
    for name in names:
        print(f"{greeting}, {name}!")

greet("Hello", "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie")
# Hello, Alice!
# Hello, Bob!
# Hello, Charlie!

items.title: Unpacking Arguments with *
items.text:
def add(a, b, c):
    return a + b + c

numbers = [1, 2, 3]
result = add(*numbers)  # Unpacks list into arguments
print(result)  # 6

# Equivalent to:
result = add(numbers[0], numbers[1], numbers[2])

items.title: **kwargs - Variable Keyword Arguments
items.text:
Collect extra keyword arguments into a dictionary:

def print_info(**kwargs):
    """Print any number of keyword arguments."""
    for key, value in kwargs.items():
        print(f"{key}: {value}")

print_info(name="Alice", age=30, city="NYC")
# name: Alice
# age: 30
# city: NYC

# kwargs is a dictionary
def show_kwargs(**kwargs):
    print(type(kwargs))  # <class 'dict'>
    print(kwargs)

show_kwargs(a=1, b=2)  # {'a': 1, 'b': 2}

items.title: Combining *args and **kwargs
items.text:
def func(a, b, *args, **kwargs):
    print(f"a={a}, b={b}")
    print(f"args={args}")
    print(f"kwargs={kwargs}")

func(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, x=10, y=20)
# a=1, b=2
# args=(3, 4, 5)
# kwargs={'x': 10, 'y': 20}

Order matters:

  1. Regular positional parameters
  2. *args
  3. Keyword-only parameters (after *)
  4. **kwargs
def func(pos1, pos2, *args, kw_only, **kwargs):
    pass

func(1, 2, 3, 4, kw_only=5, extra=6)

items.title: Unpacking Keyword Arguments with **
items.text:
def greet(name, age):
    print(f"{name} is {age} years old")

person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}
greet(**person)  # Unpacks dict into keyword arguments
# Alice is 30 years old

# Equivalent to:
greet(name=person["name"], age=person["age"])

items.title: Docstrings
items.text:
First string in function body documents the function:

def calculate_area(radius):
    """
    Calculate the area of a circle.

    Args:
        radius: The radius of the circle.

    Returns:
        The area of the circle.
    """
    return 3.14159 * radius ** 2

# Access docstring
print(calculate_area.__doc__)
help(calculate_area)

items.title: Docstring Styles
items.text:
One-line docstring:

def square(x):
    """Return the square of x."""
    return x ** 2

Multi-line docstring (Google style):

def divide(a, b):
    """
    Divide two numbers.

    Args:
        a: The numerator.
        b: The denominator.

    Returns:
        The quotient of a divided by b.

    Raises:
        ValueError: If b is zero.
    """
    if b == 0:
        raise ValueError("Cannot divide by zero")
    return a / b

Type hints in docstrings:

def greet(name: str) -> str:
    """
    Return a greeting message.

    Args:
        name: Person's name (str).

    Returns:
        Greeting message (str).
    """
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

items.title: First-Class Functions
items.text: Functions are objects - can be assigned, passed, and returned:

items.title: Assigning Functions to Variables
items.text:
def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

# Assign to variable
say_hello = greet
print(say_hello("Alice"))  # Hello, Alice!

items.title: Passing Functions as Arguments
items.text:
def apply_operation(func, value):
    """Apply a function to a value."""
    return func(value)

def square(x):
    return x ** 2

def cube(x):
    return x ** 3

result = apply_operation(square, 5)  # 25
result = apply_operation(cube, 5)    # 125

items.title: Returning Functions
items.text:
def make_multiplier(n):
    """Return a function that multiplies by n."""
    def multiplier(x):
        return x * n
    return multiplier

times_two = make_multiplier(2)
times_three = make_multiplier(3)

print(times_two(5))    # 10
print(times_three(5))  # 15

items.title: Storing Functions in Data Structures
items.text:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def subtract(a, b):
    return a - b

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

# Dictionary of functions
operations = {
    '+': add,
    '-': subtract,
    '*': multiply
}

result = operations['+'](5, 3)  # 8
result = operations['*'](5, 3)  # 15

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Functions promote code reuse, organization, and abstraction - essential for writing maintainable programs.

Defining functions:

  • def name(params): with optional return
  • Docstrings for documentation

Parameters and arguments:

  • Positional and keyword arguments
  • Default parameter values
  • *args for variable positional arguments
  • **kwargs for variable keyword arguments

Scope:

  • LEGB rule: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in
  • Use global to modify global variables

Lambda functions:

  • lambda params: expression for simple anonymous functions
  • Best for short, one-time-use functions

First-class functions:

  • Functions are objects
  • Can be assigned, passed, and returned
  • Enable functional programming patterns

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Apply functions to File Operations
  • Use with Error Handling for robust code
  • Explore advanced function concepts in Core Concepts

items.title: Definition
items.text:
A module is a Python file (.py) containing Python definitions and statements. Modules let you organize code into reusable components.

Benefits:

  • Code reusability
  • Namespace organization
  • Easier maintenance
  • Collaboration

items.title: Example Module
items.text:
math_utils.py:

"""Utility functions for mathematical operations."""

def add(a, b):
    """Return the sum of a and b."""
    return a + b

def multiply(a, b):
    """Return the product of a and b."""
    return a * b

PI = 3.14159

items.title: Basic Import
items.text:
import math

# Use module name as prefix
result = math.sqrt(16)  # 4.0
print(math.pi)          # 3.141592653589793

items.title: Import Multiple Modules
items.text:
import math
import random
import datetime

print(math.pi)
print(random.randint(1, 10))
print(datetime.datetime.now())

Or on one line (less preferred):

import math, random, datetime

items.title: From Import
items.text:
Import specific names from a module:

from math import sqrt, pi

# Use directly without module prefix
result = sqrt(16)  # 4.0
print(pi)          # 3.141592653589793

# Can't access other math functions
# math.sin(0)  # NameError - math not imported

items.title: Import All (Avoid!)
items.text:
from math import *

# Imports everything from math
result = sqrt(16)
print(pi)

Why avoid:

  • Pollutes namespace
  • Unclear where names come from
  • Risk of name conflicts
# Bad - name collision
from math import *
from numpy import *  # Both have 'sqrt', 'pi', etc.

# Which sqrt? Which pi? Unclear!
result = sqrt(16)

items.title: Import with Alias
items.text:
Rename module or function:

# Module alias
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([1, 2, 3])

# Function alias
from datetime import datetime as dt
now = dt.now()

# Avoid conflicts
from math import sqrt as math_sqrt
from numpy import sqrt as np_sqrt

result1 = math_sqrt(16)
result2 = np_sqrt([16, 25, 36])

items.title: What Happens During Import?
items.text:
1. Search for the module in sys.path
  2. Compile to bytecode (if not already cached)
  3. Execute the module code
  4. Create module object
  5. Bind name in current namespace
import my_module  # Executes my_module.py

# my_module.py executed line by line
# Top-level code runs
# Functions and classes defined
# Variables assigned

items.title: Import Caching
items.text:
Modules are imported once per program:

import my_module  # Executes my_module.py
import my_module  # Uses cached version, doesn't re-execute

# Verify: module stored in sys.modules
import sys
print('my_module' in sys.modules)  # True

items.title: Reimporting During Development
items.text:
import my_module

# Made changes to my_module.py?
import importlib
importlib.reload(my_module)  # Force re-import

items.title: The name Variable
items.text:
Every module has a __name__ attribute:

# When imported: __name__ is module name
import math
print(math.__name__)  # 'math'

# When run directly: __name__ is '__main__'
# In my_module.py:
print(__name__)
# If imported: prints 'my_module'
# If run directly: prints '__main__'

items.title: The if name == "main" Pattern
items.text:
Distinguish between import and direct execution:

my_module.py:

def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

def main():
    print(greet("World"))

# Only runs when script executed directly
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

When imported:

import my_module  # Nothing prints
result = my_module.greet("Alice")  # Use the function

When run directly:

python my_module.py  # Prints: Hello, World!

items.title: Why Use This Pattern?
items.text:
Makes modules both importable and executable:

# calculator.py
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def subtract(a, b):
    return a - b

# Test code runs only when executed directly
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"2 + 3 = {add(2, 3)}")
    print(f"5 - 2 = {subtract(5, 2)}")

As library:

import calculator
result = calculator.add(10, 5)  # Test code doesn't run

As script:

python calculator.py
# Output:
# 2 + 3 = 5
# 5 - 2 = 3

items.title: Simple Module
items.text:
greetings.py:

"""A module for greeting people."""

def hello(name):
    """Return a hello greeting."""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

def goodbye(name):
    """Return a goodbye message."""
    return f"Goodbye, {name}!"

# Module-level variable
DEFAULT_NAME = "World"

Using the module:

import greetings

print(greetings.hello("Alice"))
print(greetings.DEFAULT_NAME)

items.title: Module with Class
items.text:
person.py:

"""Person class module."""

class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

    def greet(self):
        return f"Hi, I'm {self.name}"

Using the module:

from person import Person

p = Person("Alice", 30)
print(p.greet())

items.title: Module Organization
items.text:
Good module structure:

"""
my_module.py - Brief description

Detailed explanation of what the module does.
"""

# Imports (standard library first, then third-party, then local)
import os
import sys
from datetime import datetime

# Constants
DEFAULT_VALUE = 100
MAX_RETRIES = 3

# Functions
def public_function():
    """Public function - part of module API."""
    return _private_helper()

def _private_helper():
    """Private function - internal use only."""
    # Convention: prefix with _ for "private"
    pass

# Classes
class MyClass:
    pass

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Test/demo code
    print(public_function())

items.title: sys.path
items.text:
Python searches for modules in directories listed in sys.path:

import sys
print(sys.path)
# ['', '/usr/lib/python3.9', '/usr/lib/python3.9/lib-dynload', ...]

Order:

  1. Current directory ('')
  2. PYTHONPATH environment variable
  3. Standard library
  4. Site-packages (third-party packages)

items.title: Adding to Search Path
items.text:
import sys
sys.path.append('/path/to/my/modules')

# Now can import from that directory
import my_custom_module

items.title: Relative Paths
items.text:
If module is in current directory:

my_project/
    main.py
    utils.py

main.py:

import utils  # Works - same directory

items.title: Import Organization
items.text:
PEP 8 convention:

# 1. Standard library imports
import os
import sys
from datetime import datetime

# 2. Third-party imports
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# 3. Local application imports
from my_package import my_module
from . import sibling_module  # Relative import

items.title: Avoid Circular Imports
items.text:
Problem:

module_a.py:

from module_b import func_b

def func_a():
    return func_b()

module_b.py:

from module_a import func_a

def func_b():
    return func_a()  # Circular dependency!

Solutions:

  1. Restructure code to remove circular dependency
  2. Import inside function (delayed import)
  3. Refactor common code to a third module
# Delayed import
def func_a():
    from module_b import func_b  # Import here, not at top
    return func_b()

items.title: Prefer Explicit Imports
items.text:
# Good - clear what's being used
from math import sqrt, pi, sin

# Avoid - unclear and can cause conflicts
from math import *

items.title: Use Aliases Consistently
items.text:
# Common conventions
import numpy as np        # Standard alias
import pandas as pd       # Standard alias
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # Standard alias

# Custom aliases should be meaningful
from my_long_module_name import function as fn  # OK if used often

items.title: Module Naming
items.text:
• Use lowercase
  • Use underscores for multi-word names
  • Avoid names that conflict with standard library
# Good
my_module.py
data_processor.py
utils.py

# Bad
MyModule.py      # Not PEP 8
data-processor.py  # Hyphens not allowed
string.py        # Conflicts with standard library

items.title: Public vs Private
items.text:
Use naming conventions to indicate intent:

# my_module.py

def public_function():
    """Part of module's public API."""
    pass

def _private_function():
    """Internal use only - don't import."""
    pass

# When importing
from my_module import public_function  # Good
from my_module import _private_function  # Bad practice

# Or
import my_module
my_module.public_function()  # Good
my_module._private_function()  # Possible but indicates "don't use"

items.title: Summary
items.text:
Modules in Python:

Importing:

  • import module - import entire module
  • from module import name - import specific name
  • import module as alias - import with alias
  • Avoid from module import *

The import system:

  • Modules executed on first import
  • Cached in sys.modules
  • Search path in sys.path

__name__ pattern:

  • if __name__ == "__main__": for executable modules
  • Makes code both importable and runnable

Creating modules:

  • Any .py file is a module
  • Organize code logically
  • Use docstrings
  • Follow PEP 8 conventions

Best practices:

  • Organize imports (stdlib → third-party → local)
  • Use explicit imports
  • Avoid circular dependencies
  • Use naming conventions (public vs _private)

Modules are fundamental to organizing Python code and building larger applications.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
